attrs.transmission: Skin/mucosa carriage; contact; hospital & community; foodborne (enterotoxins)
attrs.pathogenesis: Adhere → cross epithelium → evade defence → exoenzymes/toxins → local + distant injury
attrs.disease: Skin/soft-tissue (furuncles, abscess, impetigo, cellulitis, necrotizing fasciitis); bacteremia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, pneumonia; toxic: food poisoning, TSS, SSSS
attrs.virulence: Coagulase; clumping factor; Protein A; MSCRAMM; hemolysins α/β/γ/δ; PVL leucocidin; TSST-1; enterotoxins; exfoliatins
attrs.treatment: Anti-staph penicillins (PSSA); MRSA → glycopeptides (vancomycin), linezolid, daptomycin, ceftaroline, tigecycline
attrs.special: Coagulase +; MRSA = mecA→PBP2a (horizontal transfer); VISA/VRSA via vanA from enterococci

attrs.pathogenesis: Opportunistic; biofilm on indwelling devices
attrs.disease: Prosthetic/device infections, healthcare-associated infections; S. saprophyticus → UTI in young women
attrs.virulence: Biofilm (slime layer)
attrs.special: Coagulase −; opportunistic / conditional pathogens

attrs.pathogenesis: Adhesion → M-protein–fibrinogen complex → PMN integrins → endothelial damage; suppurative + immune (non-suppurative) sequelae
attrs.disease: Pharyngitis/angina, tonsillitis; impetigo, erysipelas, cellulitis; scarlet fever; invasive (necrotizing fasciitis, TSS); rheumatic fever; acute glomerulonephritis
attrs.virulence: M protein (emm) – greatest VF (anti-phagocytic, anti-complement, binds fibronectin); capsule (hyaluronic acid); streptolysin S & O; streptokinase; C5a peptidase; pyrogenic exotoxins Spe-A/B/C (superantigens)
attrs.treatment: Penicillin G + all β-lactams (still susceptible); macrolides decreased
attrs.special: β-hemolytic; rheumatic fever via molecular mimicry (M-protein ≈ cardiac myosin)

attrs.pathogenesis: Capsule + hemolysin mediated; crosses basal lamina, anti-phagocytic
attrs.disease: Neonatal sepsis/meningitis/pneumonia (early & late onset); infections in pregnant women, diabetics, elderly, immunocompromised
attrs.virulence: Capsule (10 serotypes, immunogenic); β-hemolysin; CAMP factor; C5a peptidase; α/β-C protein; hyaluronidase
attrs.special: β-hemolytic; CAMP test +; intrapartum prophylaxis prevents neonatal disease

attrs.pathogenesis: Spread nasopharynx → middle ear/sinuses/lower airway; toxin/enzyme → inflammation; invasion → systemic
attrs.disease: Non-invasive: otitis media, sinusitis, bronchitis. Invasive: pneumonia (CAP), bacteremia, meningitis
attrs.virulence: Polysaccharide capsule (main, anti-phagocytic, immunogenic); pneumolysin; PspA/PspC; IgA protease; autolysin (LytA); H₂O₂
attrs.treatment: Penicillin G / cephalosporins (resistance via PBP, murM*N); macrolides (erm/mef)
attrs.special: α-hemolytic; optochin-sensitive, bile-soluble; risk age <5/>65, asplenia, complement defect

entities.name: Other Strep (viridans, S. bovis, S. suis, S. mutans)

attrs.pathogenesis: Variable
attrs.disease: Viridans / S. bovis → endocarditis; S. suis → meningitis; S. mutans → dental caries
attrs.virulence: Adhesins; biofilm (dental)
attrs.special: Part of oral microbiota

attrs.transmission: Endogenous gut flora; nosocomial; catheters, long hospital stay, prior antibiotics
attrs.pathogenesis: Opportunistic; biofilm + adhesion → device/tissue colonization
attrs.disease: UTI; nosocomial bacteremia/septicemia, endocarditis, abdominal & biliary infections, hepatic abscess
attrs.virulence: Aggregation substance (Ace, Acm); biofilm (Esp, Ebp, Epa); capsule; gelatinase; cytolysin (Cyl); hyaluronidase
attrs.treatment: β-lactams (penicillins); naturally resistant to cephalosporins; VRE/GRE problem; fosfomycin, nitrofurantoin, linezolid/daptomycin
attrs.special: Lancefield group D; VRE = van A–L target change (D-Ala-D-Lac); intrinsic cephalosporin resistance

attrs.transmission: Soil, animal/human feces; wounds, lacerations, punctures (spores)
attrs.pathogenesis: Spore germination in anaerobic wound → tetanospasmin blocks glycine/GABA in inhibitory CNS neurons (cleaves synaptobrevin)
attrs.disease: Tetanus — spastic paralysis (generalized, localized, cephalic, neonatal)
attrs.virulence: Tetanospasmin (neurotoxin); tetanolysin
attrs.special: Spore-forming, obligate anaerobe; SPASTIC paralysis

attrs.pathogenesis: AB neurotoxin acts on peripheral cholinergic terminals → blocks ACh release → flaccid paralysis
attrs.disease: Botulism — foodborne (descending flaccid paralysis, ptosis, dysphagia, dyspnea), neonatal, wound
attrs.virulence: Botulinum neurotoxin (BoNT) — blocks ACh release
attrs.treatment: Antitoxin; ventilation; gut clearance of bacteria
attrs.special: FLACCID paralysis; Category A bioterrorism agent; large spores

attrs.pathogenesis: Food poisoning by enterotoxin strains (self-limiting); gas gangrene: trauma → entry → multiply → toxin
attrs.disease: Food poisoning; necrotizing enteritis (pigbel); gas gangrene / myonecrosis
attrs.virulence: 12 toxins (4 lethal): α (lecithinase/PLC), β, ε, ι; enterotoxin
attrs.special: β-hemolysis, capsulated, rapid growth; gas pockets, muscle necrosis

attrs.pathogenesis: Dysbiosis → ToxA/ToxB → cytoskeletal disruption, junction loss, inflammation
attrs.disease: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea; pseudomembranous colitis; toxic megacolon
attrs.virulence: TcdA (Enterotoxin A); TcdB (Cytotoxin B)
attrs.treatment: Vancomycin, metronidazole; fidaxomicin (spares flora); fecal transplant
attrs.special: Spore-forming; risk age ≥65 & prolonged antibiotics

attrs.pathogenesis: Spore entry → anthrax toxin (PA + EF + LF); manifestation depends on infection site
attrs.disease: Anthrax — cutaneous, intestinal, pulmonary
attrs.keyVF: Anthrax toxin (PA + edema factor + lethal factor)
attrs.virulence: Polypeptide capsule; anthrax toxin AB: protective antigen, edema factor (adenylate cyclase ↑cAMP), lethal factor (Zn-metalloprotease, MAPKK)
attrs.treatment: Cutaneous: Pen G/amoxicillin; pulmonary: ciprofloxacin, doxycycline; sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim
attrs.special: Category A bioterrorism agent; very resistant spores, capsulated, non-hemolytic

attrs.transmission: Contaminated food (rice, meat, veg, milk, fish)
attrs.pathogenesis: Emetic: heat-stable toxin (rice); enteric: thermolabile toxin → adenylate cyclase → ↑cAMP
attrs.disease: Emetic form → vomiting; enteric form → diarrhea (both self-limiting)
attrs.virulence: Emetic heat-stable toxin; enterotoxin (↑cAMP)
attrs.special: Spore-forming, β-hemolysis; classic 'reheated rice' food poisoning

attrs.pathogenesis: PrfA-regulated; Listeriolysin O → phagosome escape → intracellular growth + cell-to-cell spread
attrs.disease: Listeriosis: gastroenteritis, abortion/fetal infection, bacteremia, meningitis, neonatal meningoencephalitis, flu-like
attrs.virulence: Listeriolysin O; internalins/invasins; ActA
attrs.special: Grows at fridge temp (1–45°C); mortality 20–30%; risk pregnancy, neonates, elderly, immunocompromised

attrs.pathogenesis: Local skin invasion
attrs.disease: Erysipelas in animals; erysipeloid in humans
attrs.special: Facultative, asporogenous, non-capsulated

attrs.pathogenesis: Alveolar entry → macrophage phagocytosis → survives (blocks phagosome-lysosome fusion); Th cells, IFN-γ, TNF-α → granuloma (caseous necrosis); reactivation → secondary TB
attrs.disease: Tuberculosis (pulmonary); scrofula (lymph), intestinal, lupus vulgaris (skin), Pott's (bone), miliary/disseminated
attrs.keyVF: Cord factor / mycolic-acid cell wall (intracellular survival)
attrs.virulence: Cell-wall mycolic acids; cord factor; intracellular survival (anti-phagosome maturation)
attrs.treatment: 4 first-line: isoniazid, rifampicin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide; new: bedaquiline (ATP synthase); MDR/XDR/XXDR-TB
attrs.special: Acid-fast; latent vs active TB; granuloma; Dx TST/Mantoux & IGRA

attrs.pathogenesis: Th1 → tuberculoid pole (high CMI); Th2 → lepromatous pole (high humoral)
attrs.disease: Leprosy — granulomatous; skin, PNS, cooler body regions (eyes, testes, upper RT)
attrs.virulence: Intracellular persistence
attrs.treatment: Paucibacillary: dapsone + rifampicin; multibacillary: + clofazimine; reactions: prednisone
attrs.special: Acid-fast; cannot be cultured in vitro

entities.name: M. ulcerans & other MOTT (MAC, M. marinum)

attrs.pathogenesis: Mycolactone (PKS gene)
attrs.disease: M. ulcerans → Buruli ulcer; MAC → disseminated (COPD/immunocompromised)
attrs.virulence: Mycolactone
attrs.special: Acid-fast, environmental / opportunistic

attrs.pathogenesis: Local inflammation (cell-wall Ag + toxin); bloodstream diffusion of toxin → heart, nerves, kidney
attrs.disease: Diphtheria — pharyngeal pseudomembrane, cutaneous
attrs.virulence: Diphtheria toxin (phage-encoded; ADP-ribosylates EF-2)
attrs.treatment: Erythromycin / penicillin G + diphtheria antitoxin serum
attrs.special: Club-shaped rod; mycolic acids (not acid-fast); pseudomembrane formation

attrs.pathogenesis: Local pharyngeal/soft-tissue invasion
attrs.disease: Pharyngitis/pharyngotonsillitis (young adults); head/neck infections, peritonsillar abscess; skin/soft tissue
attrs.virulence: Hemolysins
attrs.special: β-hemolytic; mimics strep pharyngitis

attrs.pathogenesis: Synergy: tissue damage + opportunistic infection
attrs.disease: Actinomycosis; osteonecrosis
attrs.special: Branched rod, anaerobic; 'sulfur granules'; part of oral microbiota

attrs.pathogenesis: Wide carbon utilization; difficult to treat
attrs.disease: N. asteroides → pulmonary/disseminated; N. brasiliensis → cutaneous
attrs.virulence: Catalase, SOD (intracellular survival)
attrs.special: Filamentous/branched rod, obligate aerobe; partially acid-fast

attrs.pathogenesis: Chronic systemic; develops over years/decades
attrs.disease: Whipple disease: arthralgia, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss, fever, lymphadenopathy, anemia, CNS
attrs.special: Non-cultivable; very slow disease

attrs.transmission: M. pneumoniae: respiratory droplets; M. genitalium / M. hominis / Ureaplasma: sexual
attrs.pathogenesis: Extracellular on mucosal surface; toxic metabolites; M. pneumoniae P1 adhesin → ciliary stasis; superantigen; auto-IgM vs RBCs (cold agglutinins)
attrs.disease: M. pneumoniae → atypical pneumonia/URT; M. genitalium → urethritis; M. hominis → pyelonephritis/PID; U. urealyticum → urethritis
attrs.virulence: P1 adhesin; toxic metabolites
attrs.treatment: Macrolides, fluoroquinolones, tetracyclines; intrinsically resistant to ALL β-lactams (no cell wall); M. hominis resistant to macrolides
attrs.special: NO cell wall (no peptidoglycan) → no Gram stain, β-lactam resistant; cold agglutinins (M. pneumoniae)

attrs.pathogenesis: Adhere nasopharynx (pili/Opa) → cross BBB → blood multiplication → meningitis/meningococcemia; LPS molecular mimicry prevents C3 deposition
attrs.disease: Meningitis (+ DIC, multi-organ failure, septic shock); meningococcemia
attrs.virulence: Type IV pili; capsule (anti-phagocytic); IgA protease; Opa/Opc/Por; LOS (endotoxin); Tbp1/2, Lbp
attrs.vaccine: Yes — conjugate (A, C, Y, W135); B poorly immunogenic
attrs.special: Diplococci; capsule B mimics host (immune tolerance); risk terminal complement deficiency (C5–C9), asplenia

attrs.pathogenesis: Adhesion to non-ciliated epithelium → invasion → PMN recruitment → epithelial damage, exudate; high phase variation
attrs.disease: Gonorrhea: men urethritis (epididymitis, prostatitis); women cervicitis (PID, salpingitis); ocular, anorectal, pharyngeal, disseminated
attrs.keyVF: Type IV pili / Opa (antigenic & phase variation)
attrs.virulence: Type IV pili; Opa; Por; Rmp; LOS; Tbp1/2, Lbp
attrs.treatment: Resistant to Pen G, tetracyclines, macrolides, fluoroquinolones; 3rd-gen cephalosporin resistance rising
attrs.special: Diplococci, non-capsulated; high antigenic/phase variation; no vaccine

attrs.pathogenesis: Colonizes ciliated respiratory epithelium → toxins → local damage + systemic inflammation; phases catarrhal → paroxysmal → convalescent
attrs.disease: Pertussis (whooping cough): severe cough, seizures, encephalopathy, hypoxemia
attrs.virulence: Filamentous hemagglutinin, pertactin, fimbriae; TTSS; pertussis toxin (↑cAMP); adenylate cyclase/hemolysin; tracheal cytotoxin; dermonecrotic toxin; LOS
attrs.special: Obligate aerobe, capsulated; isolation always indicates disease

attrs.transmission: Bcc: nosocomial, contaminated disinfectants/devices; B. pseudomallei: zoonosis (soil/water)
attrs.pathogenesis: Biofilm, intracellular survival, modified LPS
attrs.disease: Bcc → bacteremia, pneumonia (cepacian syndrome) in CF/immunocompromised; B. pseudomallei → melioidosis (abscesses, pneumonia, septic shock)
attrs.virulence: Pili, 22kDa adhesin, biofilm; flagella, SOD, catalase, TSSS, hemolysin, modified LPS
attrs.treatment: MDR — resistant to most β-lactams, aminoglycosides, polymyxins
attrs.special: Major CF pathogen (B. cenocepacia); highly versatile

attrs.transmission: Endogenous flora; respiratory; risk age ≥50, smokers, lung disease
attrs.pathogenesis: Adhesion to respiratory epithelium → local then invasive disease
attrs.disease: Localized: otitis, sinusitis, conjunctivitis. Invasive: bacteremia, meningitis, endocarditis, peritonitis, pneumonia
attrs.virulence: UspA1, OMP CD, hemagglutinin; McmM, MchA1/2, OlpA/OmpJ, MacP; type IV pili; LOS endotoxin
attrs.treatment: Resistant to Pen G (β-lactamase); susceptible Pen+inhibitor, cephalosporins, macrolides; naturally resistant to vanco & clindamycin
attrs.special: Strict aerobe, no capsule; tolerates low temp (28°C); β-lactamase producer

attrs.pathogenesis: Adhesion to non-ciliated nasopharynx → IgA protease → replication; capsule → overcome barrier → invasive disease
attrs.disease: Non-capsulated: otitis media, sinusitis, bronchitis (low invasive). Encapsulated (type b): meningitis, epiglottitis, cellulitis (high invasive)
attrs.virulence: HMW1/2 adhesins, fimbriae, Hap; polysaccharide capsule (type b); IgA protease; Hb-/transferrin-binding proteins; LOS
attrs.treatment: Resistance via β-lactamases / PBP mods; chloramphenicol, rifampicin; generally susceptible
attrs.special: Needs Factor X (hemin) + V (NAD) → chocolate agar

attrs.transmission: P. multocida: animal bites; HACEK: endogenous oral flora
attrs.pathogenesis: Local then systemic spread; HACEK colonize damaged valves
attrs.disease: P. multocida → bite-wound infections, lung & systemic infections; HACEK → subacute endocarditis
attrs.virulence: Capsule, LPS
attrs.special: HACEK = Haemophilus aphrophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella; risk cardiac pathology, IV drug use, piercing

attrs.transmission: Inhalation of aerosols (water systems); risk ≥50 yrs, smokers, immunocompromised
attrs.pathogenesis: Infects macrophages (MOMP–C3b) → coiling endocytosis → type II/IV secretion, Legiobactin → inhibits phagosome-lysosome fusion → intracellular multiplication → lysis
attrs.disease: Legionellosis (Legionnaires': necrotizing multifocal pneumonia, multiorgan failure, high mortality); Pontiac fever (benign, flu-like)
attrs.virulence: MOMP; type II/IV secretion system; Legiobactin (iron)
attrs.treatment: Susceptible: macrolides, fluoroquinolones, tetracyclines; resistant: β-lactams, aminoglycosides
attrs.special: Obligate aerobe, demanding (L-cysteine + iron); replicates inside free-living amoebae

attrs.pathogenesis: Replicates in acidified Coxiella-containing vacuole; type IV secretion
attrs.disease: Q-fever: acute (high fever, headache, myalgia, pneumonia); chronic (endocarditis, hepatitis, fatigue)
attrs.virulence: Type IV secretion effectors; intracellular survival
attrs.special: Obligate intracellular; Dx immunofluorescence/ELISA

attrs.pathogenesis: EPEC intimin/Tir → villi destruction; ETEC LT/ST → ↑cAMP/cGMP; EIEC invasion (Shigella-like); EHEC Shiga-like toxin (SLT1/2, GB3 receptor); UPEC P fimbriae → urosepsis
attrs.disease: EPEC/ETEC/EAEC/DAEC → watery diarrhea; EIEC → dysentery; EHEC → hemorrhagic colitis, HUS, CNS; UPEC → UTI; MNEC → neonatal meningitis; nosocomial sepsis
attrs.virulence: LPS; fimbriae (type I, Pap, P); intimin/Tir; LT & ST; Shiga-like toxin; hemolysin; K1 capsule; secretion systems
attrs.special: Multiple pathotypes; EHEC O157:H7 → HUS (avoid antibiotics)

attrs.pathogenesis: M-cell entry → invade enterocytes → IcsA actin polymerization → cell-to-cell spread → intense inflammation
attrs.disease: Shigellosis / bacillary dysentery (blood & mucus, tenesmus, fever); sequelae HUS, Reiter syndrome
attrs.virulence: Ipa (invasion plasmid antigens); IcsA/IcsB; Shiga toxin/verotoxin (S. dysenteriae 1A; blocks 28S rRNA); ShET1/2; LPS
attrs.treatment: Rehydration; avoid antidiarrheals; severe → ampicillin, aminoglycosides, tetracyclines, chloramphenicol, fluoroquinolones
attrs.special: Lac −, strictly human, non-motile; very low infectious dose

attrs.transmission: Zoonosis; fecal-oral, foodborne; S. typhi: anthroponosis (person-to-person)
attrs.pathogenesis: M-cell/enterocyte invasion → bacteria-mediated endocytosis (SPI-1 T3SS); typhoidal survives in macrophages (SPI-2) → systemic
attrs.disease: Gastroenteritis (non-typhoidal; no lamina propria crossing); enteric fevers — typhoid/paratyphoid (crosses lamina propria, systemic)
attrs.keyVF: SPI-1 / SPI-2 type III secretion systems
attrs.virulence: SPI-1 / SPI-2 T3SS; LPS; flagella; fimbriae; Vi capsule (S. typhi)
attrs.treatment: Gastroenteritis: no antibiotics; enteric fever: chloramphenicol, ampicillin, ceftriaxone, fluoroquinolones, cotrimoxazole
attrs.special: Typhoidal vs non-typhoidal; rising resistance

attrs.pathogenesis: Capsule/biofilm-driven; hypervirulent hypermucoviscous clones
attrs.disease: RT infections, pneumonia, UTI, bacteremia/sepsis, meningitis, liver abscesses
attrs.virulence: Polysaccharide capsule (K1/K2); fimbriae; siderophores (aerobactin, yersiniabactin, salmochelin); OMP, LPS, biofilm; mpgA/rmpA/rcsA (hypermucoviscosity)
attrs.special: Non-motile, encapsulated; hypervirulent/hypermucoviscous clones

attrs.transmission: Y. pestis: rodent-flea bites, animal tissue, respiratory secretions; Y. enterocolitica: undercooked pork, milk, transfusion
attrs.pathogenesis: Plasminogen activator, T3SS/Yop → multiply & disseminate
attrs.disease: Y. pestis: bubonic / septicemic / pneumonic plague; Y. enterocolitica: enteritis, mesenteric adenitis, terminal ileitis, septicemia
attrs.virulence: F1 capsule antigen; plasminogen activator; T3SS/Yop; yersiniabactin; LPS (pMT1/pFra plasmid)
attrs.special: Y. pestis: Category A bioterrorism agent; sylvatic/urban cycle

entities.name: Vibrio cholerae (& V. vulnificus, V. parahaemolyticus)

attrs.transmission: Oro-fecal; environmental + human reservoir; V. vulnificus: contaminated water / raw shellfish
attrs.pathogenesis: Toxigenic O1/O139 → cholera toxin (CTX, phage-encoded) → massive Cl⁻/water secretion
attrs.disease: Cholera: acute profuse 'rice-water' diarrhea, dehydration, acidosis; V. vulnificus → wound/sepsis; V. parahaemolyticus → GI
attrs.virulence: Cholera toxin CTX (CTX phage); toxin-coregulated pilus (type IV); mucinases, neuraminidase, hemolysin; LPS
attrs.treatment: Rapid rehydration (oral/parenteral); antibiotics in severe forms
attrs.special: Requires NaCl; rice-water stool; VNC state; epidemic/pandemic potential

attrs.pathogenesis: Enterotoxin/hemolysin-mediated
attrs.disease: Aeromonas → GI (acute diarrhea children, chronic adults), wound, systemic (immunocompromised); Plesiomonas → self-limited diarrhea
attrs.virulence: Fimbriae, flagellum, hemolysin
attrs.special: Fresh/brackish water; water-associated

attrs.transmission: Vectors (fleas, ticks, flies, horseflies); contaminated water/food; inhalation
attrs.pathogenesis: Depends on route; systemic spread; intracellular survival
attrs.disease: Tularemia (high mortality, public-health impact)
attrs.virulence: Intracellular survival; capsule
attrs.special: Pleomorphic; easily transmitted; potential bioterror agent

attrs.pathogenesis: Multifactorial, toxin-producing & invasive; adhesion/colonization → localized/hematogenous → systemic disease
attrs.disease: Nosocomial: UTI, pneumonia, bacteremia. Community: chronic lung (CF), burns, otitis externa, keratitis, endophthalmitis, SSTIs, endocarditis
attrs.virulence: Pili/flagellum; alginate (biofilm); exotoxin A, phospholipase C (T2SS); ExoS/T/U/Y (T3SS); LasA/LasB elastase; pyoverdine/pyochelin (iron); LPS; QS (LasR/LasI); pyocyanin
attrs.special: Obligate aerobe, doesn't ferment sugars; pyocyanin/pyoverdine pigments; CF airway adaptation (mucoid/biofilm)

attrs.pathogenesis: Colonization → invasive disease in compromised hosts
attrs.disease: Ventilator pneumonia; bacteremia → septic shock; post-neurosurgical meningitis; wound & urinary infections
attrs.virulence: Resistance island (45 resistances/6 classes); biofilm; OMP
attrs.treatment: MDR/pan-drug-resistant; carbapenem resistance (AmpC, OXA, metallo-β-lactamases); colistin resistance (lipid A); tigecycline (efflux)
attrs.special: Obligate aerobe, versatile, 80% indigenous plasmids; extreme environmental resistance

attrs.pathogenesis: Opportunistic colonization in compromised hosts
attrs.disease: Bacteremia (CF, immunocompromised); pneumonia (cancer/hospitalized)
attrs.virulence: Intrinsic resistance mechanisms
attrs.treatment: MDR to most antibiotics (incl. carbapenems); co-trimoxazole often used
attrs.special: Non-fermenting; intrinsic MDR

attrs.transmission: Poorly cooked poultry, farm animals, contaminated water/dairy, sexual
attrs.pathogenesis: Corkscrew motility (flagella/TLR5) → adhesion → invasion → CDT (DNase) → cell-cycle arrest, ulceration; enterotoxins (↑cAMP); LOS
attrs.disease: Watery/hemorrhagic diarrhea, enteric fever; post-infectious Guillain-Barré syndrome
attrs.virulence: Flagella (motility); CDT; LT-like enterotoxins; LOS
attrs.special: Microaerophile, non-fermenter; Guillain-Barré sequel; corkscrew motility

attrs.pathogenesis: Urease neutralizes acid → infects antrum → inflammation → ulcer; VacA & CagA injected via T4SS
attrs.disease: Gastritis → peptic/duodenal/gastric ulcer; gastric adenocarcinoma, MALT lymphoma
attrs.virulence: Urease; polar flagella (motility); adhesin (Lewis Ag B); VacA (vacuolating toxin); CagA (cag-PAI, T4SS); LOS
attrs.treatment: Triple/quadruple therapy: amoxicillin, clarithromycin, tetracycline, metronidazole (combinations)
attrs.special: Urease + → urea breath test; only bacterium causally linked to gastric cancer

attrs.transmission: Bite/sting of arthropods (ticks, fleas, lice, mites)
attrs.pathogenesis: Invade endothelium → multiply in cytoplasm → spread → microthrombi/microhemorrhage; immune-mediated damage (LPS)
attrs.disease: Typhus group (R. typhi, R. prowazekii); spotted fever (R. rickettsii, R. conorii, R. sibirica)
attrs.virulence: pat1 (phospholipase A), tlyC, pld (phospholipase D); rickA (actin-tail); LPS
attrs.special: Coccoid-rod; obligate intracellular, non-cultivable; causes vasculitis

attrs.transmission: B. henselae: cat scratch/bite, cat fleas; Ehrlichia/Anaplasma: tick bite (deer reservoir)
attrs.pathogenesis: Ehrlichia → monocytes/macrophages; Anaplasma → neutrophils (morulae)
attrs.disease: B. henselae → cat-scratch disease, bacillary angiomatosis (HIV/AIDS); E. chaffeensis → HME; A. phagocytophilum → HGA
attrs.virulence: Intracellular survival; morula formation
attrs.special: Morulae visible in cells; tick/cat exposure

attrs.pathogenesis: Penetrates mucosa → rapid dissemination → systemic; damage largely immune-mediated
attrs.disease: Syphilis: primary chancre → secondary rash/condyloma lata → tertiary gumma/cardiovascular/neurosyphilis
attrs.virulence: Tissue adhesion; immune evasion (fibronectin coat, TprK antigenic variation); absence of LPS
attrs.special: Spiral, >3 endoflagella, anaerobe; non-cultivable; hides from immune system; Dx dark-field, VDRL, TPHA, PCR

attrs.pathogenesis: Cutaneous penetration → hematogenous dissemination; immune evasion (antigenic variation)
attrs.disease: Lyme disease (erythema migrans → joints, nervous system, heart); B. recurrentis → relapsing fever
attrs.virulence: Surface antigen variation; OspA/C
attrs.special: Spiral, 15–20 endoflagella, microaerophile; antigenic variation → relapses; Dx clinical + serology/PCR

attrs.transmission: Water/soil contaminated with animal urine (rodents, dogs, livestock)
attrs.pathogenesis: Rapid hematogenous spread → endothelial injury → hemorrhage, jaundice, renal failure
attrs.disease: Leptospirosis (hemorrhages, jaundice, renal failure, aseptic meningitis)
attrs.virulence: Motility, endothelial invasion
attrs.special: Spiral, 2 endoflagella, aerobe; zoonosis; Dx serology, PCR urine/blood

attrs.pathogenesis: Elementary body → parasite-induced phagocytosis → intracellular multiplication (cytotoxic/cytolytic); early inflammatory, late fibrotic reaction
attrs.disease: Ocular: trachoma, inclusion conjunctivitis. Genital: urethritis, cervicitis, salpingitis, PID, lymphogranuloma venereum. Neonatal: conjunctivitis, interstitial pneumonia
attrs.virulence: Intracellular survival; glycogen accumulation in inclusions
attrs.special: Obligate intracellular; dimorphic EB/RB life cycle; lack FtsZ/FtsA; chlamydia screening

attrs.transmission: C. pneumoniae: aerosols (human); C. psittaci: inhalation of dried bird feces
attrs.pathogenesis: Intracellular replication; insidious pneumonia
attrs.disease: C. pneumoniae → upper & lower respiratory infections; C. psittaci → atypical pneumonia (psittacosis)
attrs.virulence: Intracellular survival
attrs.special: Obligate intracellular; dimorphic life cycle; C. psittaci = zoonosis (birds)

questions.stem: Which of these Gram-positive genera have a LOW GC content?
questions.explanation: Low-GC Firmicutes include Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Bacillus, Clostridium, Listeria. Micrococcus and Mycobacterium are high-GC Actinobacteria.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a Gram-positive bacterium?
questions.explanation: Listeria monocytogenes is Gram-positive; the others are Gram-negative.

questions.stem: Which of these pathogenic bacteria is transmitted via the fecal–oral route?
questions.explanation: Vibrio cholerae is transmitted oro-fecally (contaminated water/food).

questions.stem: Which of these pathogenic bacteria is transmitted via the airborne route?
questions.explanation: C. diphtheriae spreads human-to-human by the airborne route.

questions.stem: The toxin responsible for toxic shock from Staphylococcus aureus is:
questions.explanation: TSST-1 is a superantigen → massive non-specific T-cell activation → cytokine storm.

questions.stem: The toxin of toxic shock of Streptococcus pyogenes is:
questions.explanation: Streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxins (Spe) act as superantigens.

questions.stem: Which of the following bacteria can cause food poisoning?
questions.explanation: S. aureus (heat-stable enterotoxins) and B. cereus (emetic/diarrheal toxins) cause food poisoning.

questions.stem: The Staphylococcus aureus PBP2a producer (MRSA):

options.t: Is resistant to all β-lactams, except the new anti-staphylococcal cephalosporins

questions.explanation: mecA→PBP2a confers resistance to virtually all β-lactams except 5th-gen anti-MRSA cephalosporins (e.g. ceftaroline); strains may also carry a penicillinase.

questions.stem: The mechanism of glycopeptide resistance in staphylococci is caused by:

options.t: Staphylococci have not developed resistance to vancomycin

questions.explanation: Glycopeptide resistance is due to target modification (D-Ala-D-Ala → D-Ala-D-Lac).

questions.stem: Which coagulase-negative staphylococcus is commonly associated with lower urinary tract infections?
questions.explanation: S. saprophyticus is a classic cause of UTI in young women.

questions.stem: Staphylococci:

options.t: The catalase test distinguishes them from each other

questions.explanation: Staphylococci are Gram-positive, catalase-positive cocci. Catalase distinguishes them from streptococci; coagulase distinguishes them from each other.

questions.stem: Enterococci:

options.t: Have the ability to form biofilms
E. faecium is usually more resistant than E. faecalis

questions.explanation: Enterococci are intrinsically cephalosporin-resistant, form biofilms, and E. faecium is generally more resistant. They are only poorly susceptible (low-level) to aminoglycosides, not fully resistant.

questions.stem: Streptococci:

options.t: Are classified based on their hemolytic characteristics

questions.explanation: Streptococci are Gram-positive, catalase-negative, and classified by hemolysis (α/β/γ) and Lancefield group.

questions.stem: Which of the following are α-hemolytic streptococci?
questions.explanation: S. pneumoniae and viridans group are α-hemolytic; S. pyogenes and S. agalactiae are β-hemolytic.

questions.stem: Which of the following are β-hemolytic streptococci?
questions.explanation: S. pyogenes (group A) and S. agalactiae (group B) are β-hemolytic.

questions.stem: Which Streptococcus is responsible for glomerulonephritis?
questions.explanation: Post-streptococcal acute glomerulonephritis follows S. pyogenes (GAS) infection.

questions.stem: The disease known as "pigbel" (severe necrotizing enteritis) is caused by:
questions.explanation: Pigbel is necrotizing enteritis caused by C. perfringens (β-toxin).

questions.stem: Pseudomembranous colitis is supported by:
questions.explanation: C. difficile (ToxA/ToxB) causes pseudomembranous colitis.

questions.stem: The disease caused by Clostridium tetani is associated with:
questions.explanation: Tetanospasmin blocks inhibitory neurotransmitters → spastic paralysis.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about botulinum toxin are true?
questions.explanation: BoNT is a neurotoxin that blocks ACh release → flaccid paralysis.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about tetanus toxin are true?
questions.explanation: Tetanospasmin is a neurotoxin causing spastic paralysis.

questions.stem: The anthrax toxin produced by Bacillus anthracis is:
questions.explanation: Anthrax toxin = protective antigen + edema factor + lethal factor (3 components).

questions.stem: Which is the most common form of anthrax in humans?

options.t: All three forms are equally common

questions.explanation: Cutaneous anthrax is by far the most common form.

questions.stem: The diphtheria vaccine consists of:
questions.explanation: The diphtheria vaccine is a toxoid (anatoxin = inactivated toxin).

questions.stem: Which statements about Corynebacterium diphtheriae are correct?
questions.explanation: C. diphtheriae is Gram-positive, forms a pharyngeal pseudomembrane, produces an AB EXOtoxin, and is non-invasive (disease is toxin-mediated).

questions.stem: Mycobacteria:

options.t: Do not grow on ordinary culture media

questions.explanation: Mycobacteria have a mycolic-acid (lipid-rich) wall, are acid-fast, and do not grow on ordinary media; most environmental mycobacteria are not human pathogens.

questions.stem: A positive PPD/tuberculin (Mantoux) skin test is an expression of:

options.t: A cell-mediated response against M. tuberculosis
A humoral response against M. tuberculosis
A humoral and cell-mediated response against M. tuberculosis

questions.explanation: The Mantoux test reflects a cell-mediated (delayed-type) immune response.

questions.stem: Which statement about Mycobacterium tuberculosis is correct?

options.t: In most cases, primary infection does not progress to disease
Specific antibodies play a protective role
A positive Mantoux test always indicates active disease
The BCG vaccine is prepared from a virulent strain of M. tuberculosis

questions.explanation: Most primary infections are contained (latent TB); antibodies are not protective; BCG uses an attenuated M. bovis strain.

questions.stem: The Nocardia:

options.t: Can cause pulmonary, skin and disseminated infections, particularly in the immunocompromised

questions.explanation: All statements are true → Nocardia are aerobic, filamentous actinomycetes causing disease in immunocompromised hosts.

questions.stem: Which statements regarding Neisseria meningitidis are correct?

options.t: It is a Gram-negative, oxidase-positive diplococcus
It has endotoxin (LOS) in the cell wall responsible for damage
It produces an exotoxin that stimulates adenylate cyclase
It has an antiphagocytic polysaccharide capsule

questions.explanation: N. meningitidis is a Gram-negative oxidase-positive diplococcus with LOS endotoxin and an antiphagocytic capsule; it does not produce an adenylate-cyclase exotoxin.

questions.stem: Which statements regarding Neisseria gonorrhoeae are correct?

options.t: It has type IV pili necessary for adhesion
It does not have a polysaccharide capsule

questions.explanation: N. gonorrhoeae has type IV pili, lacks a polysaccharide capsule, and is sexually transmitted; it is essentially always pathogenic but may be asymptomatic — and unlike N. meningitidis it is not part of the normal flora.

questions.stem: Which bacterial genus requires hemin (factor X) and/or NAD (factor V) for growth in vitro?
questions.explanation: Haemophilus requires factor X (hemin) and factor V (NAD) → grows on chocolate agar.

questions.stem: Enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli (EHEC):

options.t: Causes dysentery similar to bacillary dysentery
Produces an LT or an ST
Produces a Shiga-like toxin and can cause hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS)

questions.explanation: EHEC (e.g. O157:H7) produces Shiga-like toxin → hemorrhagic colitis and HUS.

questions.stem: Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC):

options.t: Causes dysentery similar to bacillary dysentery
Causes a secretory diarrhea similar to cholera but milder
Produces an LT, an ST, or both

questions.explanation: ETEC produces heat-labile (LT) and/or heat-stable (ST) toxins → watery (cholera-like, milder) diarrhea.

questions.stem: Which statements about Vibrio cholerae are correct?

options.t: It produces an AB enterotoxin responsible for the clinical manifestations
It can enter the VNC (viable-but-non-culturable) phase
It is transmitted via the fecal–oral route

questions.explanation: V. cholerae has TWO chromosomes, can enter the VNC state, is transmitted oro-fecally, and produces the AB cholera toxin (CTX).

questions.stem: Which of the following virulence factors are produced by Helicobacter pylori?
questions.explanation: H. pylori produces urease, polar flagella, VacA and CagA; leukocidin is not an H. pylori factor.

questions.stem: Legionella pneumophila:

options.t: Is transmitted through the aerosol generated by water pipes
Is transmitted through ingestion of contaminated food or beverages

questions.explanation: Legionella is inhaled from aerosolized water and causes atypical pneumonia; it is fastidious (needs L-cysteine + iron) and slow-growing.

questions.stem: The first stage of infection with Bordetella pertussis is:

options.t: Colonization of the ciliated respiratory epithelium
Colonization of the non-ciliated respiratory epithelium

questions.explanation: B. pertussis first colonizes the ciliated respiratory epithelium.

questions.stem: The etiologic agent most often responsible for non-gonococcal / post-gonococcal urethritis is:
questions.explanation: Chlamydia trachomatis is the leading cause of non-gonococcal urethritis.

questions.stem: What is the main route of transmission of Chlamydia trachomatis?
questions.explanation: C. trachomatis genital infection is mainly sexually transmitted.

questions.stem: Treponema pallidum:

options.t: Can be transmitted sexually and transplacentally

questions.explanation: T. pallidum cannot be cultured in vitro, is transmitted sexually/transplacentally, and remains penicillin-sensitive.

questions.stem: Which disease is caused by Borrelia burgdorferi?
questions.explanation: B. burgdorferi causes Lyme disease (Ixodes tick).

questions.stem: Which of the following human pathogens is anaerobic?
questions.explanation: Bacteroides fragilis is an obligate anaerobe.

questions.stem: Which is the main reservoir of Leptospira infection?
questions.explanation: Rodents are the main reservoir; humans acquire it via water/soil contaminated with animal urine.

questions.stem: Which of the following bacteria is NOT a nosocomial pathogen?
questions.explanation: Shigella is a community fecal-oral pathogen, not a typical nosocomial one; the others are classic nosocomial agents.

questions.stem: The infectious form of Chlamydia is:
questions.explanation: The elementary body (EB) is the infectious, extracellular form; the reticulate body replicates intracellularly.

questions.stem: In which of the following bacterial diseases can a chronic carrier state develop?
questions.explanation: Chronic carriage is described for C. diphtheriae, N. meningitidis and typhoidal Salmonella; Shigella does not establish a chronic carrier state.

questions.stem: Clinical clues — Gram-negative rod, oxidase-negative, frequently associated with urinary tract infections, both community-acquired and nosocomial. Which pathogen?
questions.explanation: Gram-negative, oxidase-negative, leading UTI agent (community + nosocomial) = E. coli (Pseudomonas is oxidase-positive).

questions.stem: Clinical clues — Gram-negative helical bacterium, associated with poultry and travel, self-limiting gastroenteritis (occasionally persistent), possible development of Guillain-Barré syndrome. Most likely pathogen?
questions.explanation: Helical Gram-negative, poultry/travel, Guillain-Barré sequel → Campylobacter jejuni.
